summary: DHH considers purchasing a MySQL Network support package for 37signals but finds the marketing descriptions vague and unhelpful. He criticizes the enterprise-speak used to describe the offering, noting it's impossible to tell whether you're getting real value or just a restart script and newsletter. Rather than relying on the marketing copy, he turns to the 'lazyweb' — his blog readers — to ask for real-world experiences with MySQL Network support packages.
key_points: MySQL's marketing copy is too vague to evaluate the actual value of their support packages
Enterprise marketing language obscures rather than clarifies what customers actually receive
DHH wants to support MySQL as an open source company but needs evidence the support is worthwhile
Crowdsourcing real user experiences is more valuable than parsing corporate marketing
money_quotes: From that bowl of marketing, it's hard to judge whether you're just signing up for an expensive restart script and a newsletter, or if you're actually getting something of value.
I reckon the fine engineers at MySQL AB might have left the job of describing their offerings to someone else.
I'd love to hand these chaps some cash if they're as good as you could hope an open source company could be.
key_insight: Enterprise marketing copy that obscures what you're actually buying is a red flag, and real user testimonials are worth more than any product description.
